common_name: TAM Jalapeño Pepper (Texas A&M Mild Jalapeño)

planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; TAM Jalapeño was developed by Texas A&M University in 1992 in partnership with USDA to produce a jalapeño pepper with dramatically reduced heat (400–1,500 SHU versus the standard jalapeño's 2,500–8,000 SHU) while retaining the characteristic jalapeño flavor, size, and appearance — important for the food industry and gardeners who want jalapeño flavor without significant heat.

directions: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant outdoors 2 weeks after last frost into warm, well-drained soil in full sun. TAM Jalapeño-1 was developed by Texas A&M University's pepper breeding program in cooperation with the USDA and released in 1992 specifically for the commercial food processing industry, which required a jalapeño with the characteristic jalapeño flavor, uniform size (3–4 inches), and appearance for use in products like jalapeño poppers, nachos, and salsas — but without enough heat to cause consumer complaints when consumed in quantity. The resulting variety has the same thick, meaty walls, dark green color, and characteristic jalapeño aroma as standard jalapeños but only 400–1,500 SHU (compared to 2,500–8,000 SHU in standard varieties), making it easily tolerable for most heat-sensitive individuals. TAM Jalapeño is an outstanding choice for Gulf Coast home gardeners who want to grow their own jalapeños for cooking without excessive heat, and for making fresh salsa, pico de gallo, and pickled jalapeños accessible to children and heat-sensitive family members.

common_name: NuMex Big Jim Pepper (World's Longest Chile Pepper)

planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; NuMex Big Jim was released in 1975 by New Mexico State University (NMSU) plant breeder Dr. Roy Nakayama and holds the Guinness World Record for the longest chile pepper — individual fruits can reach 12–13 inches — it is the standard New Mexico/Hatch green chile for roasting, stuffing, and making authentic red and green New Mexico chile sauces.

directions: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant into warm, well-drained soil with consistent moisture for maximum fruit size; NuMex Big Jim is most famous when roasted over open flame or under a broiler in the New Mexico tradition. NuMex Big Jim was developed at New Mexico State University's Chile Pepper Institute by Dr. Roy Nakayama and released in 1975 as a breakthrough in Hatch/New Mexico green chile breeding; it holds the Guinness World Record for the world's longest chile pepper, with individual fruits measured up to 13 inches in length and 2–2.5 inches in diameter — fruit of this size are capable of being stuffed whole (for chile rellenos) without cutting, which drove the commercial appeal. The heat level is moderate (500–2,500 SHU — from virtually nil to mild-moderate depending on growing conditions, with hotter summers producing hotter fruit), characteristic of New Mexico green chiles rather than the sharp heat of jalapeños or serranos. The flavor when roasted is rich, smoky, earthy, and complex with characteristic New Mexico chile character — the basis of authentic Hatch green chile sauce, the iconic topping of New Mexico cuisine from green chile cheeseburgers to burritos and sopapillas. One of the most important American regional chile varieties.
sources: New Mexico State University Chile Pepper Institute

common_name: Bishop's Crown Pepper (Aji Cristal, Christmas Bell Pepper)

planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; Bishop's Crown belongs to Capsicum baccatum (the South American Aji species group), requiring slightly warmer germination temperatures (78–88°F) and a longer season than C. annuum peppers; produces dramatically unique three-winged, bishop's miter-shaped fruits with mild heat (1,500–5,000 SHU) that are sweet and fruity in the wings with heat concentrated at the center.

directions: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost (or up to 12 weeks for best transplant size); Bishop's Crown peppers are longer-season than most C. annuum peppers and benefit from the long warm seasons of the Gulf Coast and Southern gardens. Bishop's Crown (also marketed as 'Aji Cristal,' 'Christmas Bell,' and 'Balloon Bell' in various countries) is a Capsicum baccatum pepper from South America with a uniquely dramatic three-lobed shape resembling the miter (hat) worn by Catholic bishops or a flying saucer viewed from below — three triangular wings radiate outward from the base of the fruit, giving it a shape unlike any other pepper. The fruits ripen from green through yellow to orange-red; the three wings are relatively mild and sweet (1,500–2,500 SHU in the flanges) while the flesh around the central cavity is hotter (3,000–5,000 SHU). The flavor profile is characteristic of C. baccatum peppers — fruity, slightly floral, and complex with citrus undertones different from the sharper heat of jalapeños. Outstanding as an edible ornamental (the distinctive shape makes the plant conversation-worthy), in salads where the three-winged shape is visually spectacular, and lightly pickled or stuffed.
sources: New Mexico State University Chile Pepper Institute

planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; NuMex Twilight was developed at New Mexico State University's Chile Pepper Institute as a compact ornamental-edible pepper producing upright, small fruits that ripen through an extraordinary sequence of colors — purple, yellow, orange, and red — all simultaneously visible on a single plant, resembling a fiesta of colors inspired by the Southwestern twilight sky.

directions: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; NuMex Twilight is compact enough for containers (8–12 inches) and makes an outstanding patio plant both for ornamental color and edible use. NuMex Twilight was developed at New Mexico State University's internationally renowned Chile Pepper Institute as part of their ornamental-edible pepper breeding program; the variety takes its name from the brilliant multicolor sky of a New Mexico twilight that it is said to evoke — each plant produces upright clusters of small (1–2 inch), conical fruits that transition through the colors purple (immature) → yellow → orange → red (fully ripe), with all four colors simultaneously visible on a mature, productive plant. The heat level is approximately 50,000–75,000 SHU — significant but not extreme, in the range of cayenne peppers — and the small fruits are fully edible at any color stage; purple fruits are mildest, red fruits are hottest. As an ornamental plant, NuMex Twilight is among the most stunning of all edible plants, combining constant visual interest through the growing season with genuine culinary utility for dried chile flakes, hot sauces, and fresh use. Outstanding in containers, as a border plant, or as an annual focal point in vegetable and ornamental beds.
sources: New Mexico State University Chile Pepper Institute

common_name: Mucho Nacho Jalapeño Pepper (Extra-Large AAS Jalapeño)

planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; Mucho Nacho is an All-America Selections award-winning jalapeño variety producing exceptionally large fruits (4–5 inches long versus standard jalapeño's 2–3 inches) with thick, meaty walls and heat in the 10,000–12,000 SHU range — hotter than TAM Jalapeño but similar in shape, designed for nachos and poppers where large size matters.

directions: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant outdoors 2 weeks after last frost in warm, fertile, well-drained soil; Mucho Nacho is a vigorous, productive plant well-suited to Gulf Coast and Southern growing seasons. Mucho Nacho is an All-America Selections (AAS) award-winning jalapeño developed specifically to produce extra-large (4–5 inch long), thick-walled jalapeño fruits that are large enough for easy stuffing for jalapeño poppers and have enough surface area for maximum nacho topping use — the name directly references its intended use. The heat level (10,000–12,000 SHU) is somewhat hotter than many standard jalapeño varieties and significantly hotter than TAM Jalapeño, making it authentic jalapeño heat in a much larger package. The very thick walls (up to 1/4 inch) make Mucho Nacho excellent for grilling whole (the thick walls hold up without burning through) and for stuffing with cream cheese or other fillings. Ripens from dark green to red; can be harvested and used at the green stage (most common) or allowed to ripen red for a slightly different, sweeter flavor. High productivity and disease resistance make it one of the most reliable jalapeño varieties for home garden use.
